data.content:
Clinical Primary Prevention
Clinical Primary Prevention is the initial stage of care where foundational lifestyle changes are emphasized.

Therapeutic Lifestyle Changes (TLC)
TLC is a foundation for clinical primary prevention that stresses reductions in saturated fat and cholesterol intakes.<SEP>TLC is a multifaceted lifestyle approach recommended by ATP III to reduce risk for CHD, involving dietary changes, increased physical activity, and weight reduction.

LDL Cholesterol
LDL cholesterol is a major target of cholesterol-lowering therapy; elevated levels are associated with Coronary Heart Disease.<SEP>LDL Cholesterol levels are the primary focus of risk assessment, determining goals for lowering therapy and classification risk.<SEP>LDL cholesterol is the primary target of therapy for reducing long-term risk, and its levels guide the intensity of risk-reduction therapy.<SEP>LDL cholesterol is a critical measured level that, when high or elevated, requires attention and monitoring.<SEP>LDL cholesterol is a key biomarker measured in persons, used to determine therapeutic requirements and risk levels.<SEP>A key biomarker that the TLC Diet aims to reduce, as its management is crucial for cardiovascular health.<SEP>Low-Density Lipoprotein, which is targeted for reduction to levels below 100 mg/dL in secondary prevention.

LDL-lowering Drugs
LDL-lowering drugs are a therapeutic option used for persons at higher risk to reduce major coronary events and coronary death.<SEP>LDL-lowering drugs are medications used to reduce LDL cholesterol levels in patients at higher risk.<SEP>LDL-lowering drugs are medical treatments considered for persons whose LDL cholesterol levels are elevated to achieve specific risk reduction goals.

Major Coronary Events
Major coronary events are severe cardiac incidents whose risk is reduced by LDL-lowering drugs.

Coronary Death
Coronary death is a type of cardiac fatality whose risk is reduced by LDL-lowering drugs.

Secondary Dyslipidemia
Secondary dyslipidemia is an abnormal lipid condition that must be ruled out before starting lipid-lowering therapy.

Hyperlipidemia
Hyperlipidemia is a general condition characterized by elevated lipid levels.

Diabetes
Diabetes is treated as a CHD risk equivalent within the ATP III classification guidelines.<SEP>Diabetes is identified as a potential cause of secondary dyslipidemia.

Hypothyroidism
Hypothyroidism is identified as a potential cause of secondary dyslipidemia.

Obstructive Liver Disease
Obstructive liver disease is identified as a potential cause of secondary dyslipidemia.

Chronic Renal Failure
Chronic renal failure is identified as a potential cause of secondary dyslipidemia.

Progestins, Anabolic Steroids, Corticosteroids
These drug types can increase LDL cholesterol and decrease HDL cholesterol, potentially causing secondary dyslipidemia.

Secondary Prevention
Secondary prevention focuses on managing and reducing risk for persons with established CHD.<SEP>The objective of drug therapy for CHD and CHD risk equivalents, aiming to attain low LDL cholesterol.

Total Mortality
Total mortality is one of the outcomes reduced by LDL-lowering therapy in persons with established CHD.

Coronary Heart Disease (CHD)
CHD is a major cardiovascular risk that is strongly associated with elevated LDL cholesterol.<SEP>CHD is the established cardiovascular disease state for which LDL-lowering therapy is crucial.<SEP>A cardiovascular condition that requires secondary prevention and management of lipid risk factors.<SEP>CHD is a disease whose age-adjusted mortality rates declined in the United States between 1970 and 2000.

ATP III
ATP III is a set of classification guidelines used for determining goals and modalities for LDL-lowering therapy based on various risk factors.<SEP>ATP III is a body that specifies an optimal LDL cholesterol goal of <100 mg/dL for secondary prevention.<SEP>ATP III is an assessment protocol that uses major risk factors to determine the core risk status.

Metabolic Syndrome
Metabolic syndrome is a set of multiple metabolic risk factors that identify persons for intensive therapeutic lifestyle changes.<SEP>A condition constituted by a constellation of major risk factors, life-habit risk factors, and emerging risk factors, characterized by abdominal obesity and dyslipidemia.<SEP>Metabolic syndrome is a cluster of risk factors, including elevated triglyceride or low HDL cholesterol.<SEP>Metabolic Syndrome is a health condition that requires attention to other lipid and nonlipid risk factors.<SEP>A health syndrome that requires management, with weight reduction being a primary therapeutic goal.<SEP>Metabolic Syndrome is a cluster of conditions that must be addressed within the framework of hypertension management.<SEP>Metabolic Syndrome is a condition whose estimated prevalence and relationship to adult treatment panels are analyzed in the provided tables.

Saturated Fat and Cholesterol
These are dietary components that the TLC diet recommends reducing.

LDL Cholesterol Goal
The LDL Cholesterol Goal represents specific cutpoints used to determine the necessary intensity of lipid-lowering therapy.

TLC Diet
The TLC Diet is a dietary regimen that stresses reductions in saturated fat and cholesterol intakes.<SEP>TLC Diet is a recommended first-line therapeutic approach involving dietary modifications aimed at managing LDL cholesterol.<SEP>The TLC Diet is a structured nutritional intervention designed to manage cardiovascular health by controlling specific nutrient intakes.

Optimal LDL Cholesterol Level
An LDL cholesterol level of <100 mg/dL is considered optimal for secondary prevention.

Total Cholesterol
Total cholesterol is a measured component of the lipoprotein profile recommended for initial testing.<SEP>Total Cholesterol levels are used in classification to determine risk categories and guide management goals.<SEP>Total cholesterol is a risk factor used in the Framingham scoring system for 10-year risk assessment.

HDL Cholesterol
HDL cholesterol is a measured component of the lipoprotein profile, with classification recommendations updated in ATP III.<SEP>HDL Cholesterol levels are used in classification to determine risk categories and guide management goals.<SEP>HDL cholesterol is a component of atherogenic dyslipidemia and is recognized as a factor in metabolic syndrome.<SEP>HDL cholesterol is a biomarker, and low levels suggest the need for specific lipid-modifying treatments.<SEP>HDL cholesterol is a biomarker that can be raised by consuming a higher intake of unsaturated fat.<SEP>High-Density Lipoprotein, which is targeted for an increase to levels of 3-5%.

CHD
CHD stands for coronary heart disease, representing the major health condition whose risk level is being assessed and modified.<SEP>CHD stands for Coronary Heart Disease and represents the long-term risk outcome targeted by therapeutic interventions.

Framingham Risk Scores
The Framingham risk scores are a method used to estimate the 10-year CHD risk for persons with multiple risk factors.

Cigarette Smoking
Cigarette smoking is listed as a major risk factor that contributes to the modification of LDL goals.<SEP>Cigarette smoking is identified as a major, modifiable risk factor for CHD.

Hypertension
Hypertension is a major risk factor, defined by a blood pressure reading of $\ge$ 140/90 mmHg or use of antihypertensive medication.<SEP>Hypertension is the central condition being evaluated and managed throughout the document.<SEP>Hypertension is the specific focus of the committee's reports, detailing its causes, classifications, and various forms of treatment.<SEP>Hypertension is the primary medical condition addressed throughout the report, concerning high blood pressure.<SEP>Hypertension is the medical condition the guidelines aim to prevent, detect, and treat.<SEP>Hypertension is a common medical condition whose prevalence, awareness, treatment, and control were monitored by national surveys and global organizations.<SEP>Hypertension is a common condition in the United States, often uncontrolled and undiagnosed, which places strain on healthcare.

Family History of Premature CHD
This is a major risk factor identified by the occurrence of CHD in a first-degree relative under specific age limits.

Age
Age is a major risk factor used in conjunction with other measurements to assess 10-year CHD risk.<SEP>Age is recognized as a major risk factor for CHD in the Framingham scoring system.<SEP>Age in years of the patient.

Atherosclerotic Disease
Atherosclerotic disease includes other clinical forms such as peripheral arterial disease, abdominal aortic aneurysm, and symptomatic carotid artery disease.

Peripheral Arterial Disease
Peripheral arterial disease is cited as one of the clinical forms of atherosclerotic disease.<SEP>This topic addresses peripheral arterial disease as a component of metabolic health risks.

Abdominal Aortic Aneurysm
Abdominal aortic aneurysm is cited as one of the clinical forms of atherosclerotic disease.

Symptomatic Carotid Artery Disease
Symptomatic carotid artery disease is cited as one of the clinical forms of atherosclerotic disease.

data.entity_name: National Heart, Lung, and Blood Institute
data.content:
National Heart, Lung, and Blood Institute
The National Heart, Lung, and Blood Institute (NHLBI) is a federal entity providing guidelines and being subject to public laws regarding non-discrimination.<SEP>The National Heart, Lung, and Blood Institute is a member contributing to the report.<SEP>This institute is one of the member organizations contributing to the report.<SEP>This Institute supported the work and provided financial backing for the report.<SEP>The National Heart, Lung, and Blood Institute is a major federal institute concerning cardiovascular and pulmonary health.<SEP>The National Heart, Lung, and Blood Institute is the institution that prepared and published reports on cardiovascular, lung, and blood diseases.<SEP>This institute prepared data detailing the percent decline in age-adjusted mortality rates for stroke and coronary heart disease.

National Cholesterol Education Program
The National Cholesterol Education Program is the body responsible for creating updated clinical guidelines for high blood cholesterol.<SEP>The National Cholesterol Education Program is an expert panel responsible for guidelines on the detection, evaluation, and treatment of high blood cholesterol in adults.

National Institutes of Health
The National Institutes of Health (NIH) is a governmental organization associated with publishing the guidelines.<SEP>The National Institutes of Health provided support for the production of the guideline reports.

High Blood Cholesterol
High blood cholesterol is a medical condition that is the focus of updated clinical guidelines for detection and treatment.<SEP>High Blood Cholesterol is the medical condition that the NCEP Expert Panel addressed in its reports.

Adult Treatment Panel III
Adult Treatment Panel III is the name given to the third expert panel report by the National Cholesterol Education Program.<SEP>This panel is used in the clinical criteria defining the metabolic syndrome.

Congress
Congress is the legislative body whose laws mandate non-discrimination provisions in the United States.

Executive Order 11141
Executive Order 11141 prohibits discrimination based on age for contractors and subcontractors performing Federal contracts.

Executive Order 11246
Executive Order 11246 prohibits federally funded contractors from discriminating against employees or applicants based on protected characteristics.

Scott M. Grundy
Scott M. Grundy is listed as the Chair of the National Cholesterol Education Program Expert Panel.

James I. Cleeman
James I. Cleeman served as the Executive Director and was the Coordinator for the National Heart, Lung, and Blood Institute.

Theodore G. Ganiats
Theodore G. Ganiats is a representative of the American Academy of Family Physicians and a member of the Coordinating Committee.

American Academy of Family Physicians
The American Academy of Family Physicians is a member organization comprising a representative on the Coordinating Committee.<SEP>This academy is listed as a representative member organization.

Third Report of the National Cholesterol Education Expert Panel on Detection, Evaluation, and Treatment of High Blood Cholesterol in Adults
The Third Report of the Expert Panel details the detection, evaluation, and treatment of high blood cholesterol in adults.

data.entity_name: Third Report of the Expert Panel on Detection, Evaluation, and Treatment of High Blood Cholesterol in Adults (Adult Treatment Panel III) (ATP III)
data.content:
Third Report of the Expert Panel on Detection, Evaluation, and Treatment of High Blood Cholesterol in Adults (Adult Treatment Panel III) (ATP III)
ATP III constitutes updated clinical guidelines for cholesterol testing and management, building on previous ATP reports.

Triglycerides
Triglycerides are a measured component of the lipoprotein profile, requiring attention if moderately elevated.<SEP>Triglycerides are a lipid risk factor that can be reduced by consuming total fat, especially unsaturated fat.<SEP>Measures the level of TG, which is targeted for reduction (e.g., below 200 mg/dL).

Lipoprotein Profile
The Lipoprotein Profile is the preferred initial test that measures total cholesterol, LDL, HDL, and triglycerides.

Framingham Projections
Framingham projections utilize 10-year absolute CHD risk to identify patients with multiple risk factors needing intensive treatment.

ATP I
ATP I outlined a strategy for primary prevention of Coronary Heart Disease (CHD) in persons with high or borderline-high LDL cholesterol.

ATP II
ATP II affirmed the importance of primary prevention and added the intensive management of LDL cholesterol in persons with established CHD.

Plant Stanols/Sterols
Plant stanols/sterols are recommended therapeutic dietary options for enhancing the lowering of LDL cholesterol.<SEP>Plant stanols/sterols are a therapeutic option for enhancing LDL lowering, recommended at a dosage of 2 g/day.<SEP>These are therapeutic options for lowering LDL cholesterol, recommended for adding to the diet if goals are not met.

Viscous Fiber
Viscous (soluble) fiber is recommended as a therapeutic dietary option for lowering LDL cholesterol.<SEP>Viscous (soluble) fiber is a dietary element recommended for enhancing LDL lowering, with a target intake of 10-25 g/day.

Nicotinic Acid
Nicotinic Acid is an example of a lipid-modifying drug that can be used if a patient has elevated triglyceride or low HDL cholesterol.<SEP>A drug available in immediate, extended, and sustained release forms, used in lipid and metabolic management.

Fibric Acid
Fibric Acid is an example of a lipid-modifying drug used in the treatment of other lipid or nonlipid risk factors.

Triglyceride
Triglyceride is a biomarker, and elevated levels suggest the need for specific lipid-modifying treatments.

10-Year Risk
The 10-Year Risk is a calculated measure used to determine the intensity of required therapy for managing cardiovascular risk.

Coronary Atherosclerosis
Coronary Atherosclerosis is a development that drug therapy aims to slow down, reducing long-term risk.

Saturated Fats
Saturated fats are a nutrient that should be reduced in intake, specifically recommended to be less than 7% of total calories.

Cholesterol
Cholesterol is a key nutrient marker and dietary component, with recommended intake levels guidelines provided for dietary management.<SEP>Cholesterol intake must be kept below 200 mg/day as part of the TLC Diet.

Clinical Judgment
Clinical judgment is required when applying therapy guidelines, particularly for older persons, to consider various factors like concomitant illnesses and general health status.

Statin Drugs
A class of drugs, including lovastatin, pravastatin, simvastatin, fluvastatin, atorvastatin, and cerivastatin, used in lipid management.

Bile Acid Sequestrants
A class of drugs available for lowering LDL cholesterol, which binds with bile acids in the gastrointestinal tract.<SEP>A class of agents, including cholestyramine, colestipol, and colesevelam, used to reduce major intestinal bile acid secretion.

Hepatotoxicity
A potential side effect or risk associated with drug usage.

Food and Drug Administration (FDA)
The governmental body responsible for allowing medications to become over-the-counter (OTC) drugs.

Peptic Ulcer Disease
A potential gastrointestinal complication associated with drug use.

Dyspepsia
Indigestion symptoms that can result from the use of various drugs.

Myopathy
A muscular condition listed as a potential complication of certain drug types.

Cytochrome P-450 Inhibitors
A category of drugs that requires caution when used with fibrates and niacin due to metabolic interactions.

Fibrates
A class of drugs, including gemfibrozil, fenofibrate, and clofibrate, used in lipid management.

Hyperuricemia
Elevated uric acid levels, which are associated with the risk of severe gout.

Gastrointestinal Distress
General discomfort or issues related to the digestive tract, specifically mentioned in relation to bile acid binding.

Viscous (Soluble) Fiber
This nutrient is recommended for dietary intake to help lower LDL cholesterol.

Saturated Fat
Saturated fat intake must be reduced significantly when initiating the TLC Diet.<SEP>Reduced intake of saturated fat is a key component of the public health approach to primary prevention.

Polyunsaturated Fat
Polyunsaturated fat is a nutrient that the TLC Diet recommends maintaining up to 10% of total calories.

Monounsaturated Fat
Monounsaturated fat is a nutrient that the TLC Diet recommends maintaining up to 20% of total calories.

Total Fat
Total fat intake is allowed to range from 25-35% of total calories, provided saturated fats are low.

Carbohydrate
Carbohydrate intake should account for 50-60% of total calories and should predominantly come from complex sources.

Fiber
Fiber intake is recommended at 20-30 g/day as part of the TLC Diet.

Protein
Protein intake should account for approximately 15% of total calories.

Total Calories (Energy)
The goal regarding total calories is to balance energy intake and expenditure to maintain desirable body weight.

Overweight And Obese
This is a state of the population that requires weight reduction therapy and comprehensive management.

Weight Reduction Therapy
Weight reduction therapy is recommended for overweight or obese patients, enhancing LDL lowering and overall health.

Dietary Guidelines For Americans 2000
These guidelines provide the foundational recommendations for the TLC Diet, with one exception for total fat range.

Physical Activity
Physical activity, including moderate activity, is encouraged to improve overall health and assist in weight management.

HMG CoA Reductase Inhibitors
A class of drugs available for lowering LDL cholesterol, such as statins, with various potential side effects.

Registered Dietitians
Registered Dietitians are qualified nutrition professionals who are recommended for medical nutrition therapy.

Complex Carbohydrates
Complex carbohydrates, which include whole grains, fruits, and vegetables, are the preferred source for carbohydrate intake.

Transfatty Acids
Transfatty acids are a type of fat that raises LDL cholesterol and must be kept at a low intake.

Nutritionist
A qualified nutrition professional, who, along with registered dietitians, can provide medical nutrition therapy.

Framingham Scoring
Framingham Scoring is a method used for 10-year risk assessment for Coronary Heart Disease (CHD) that considers multiple risk factors.

Blood Pressure
Blood pressure is listed as a major risk factor used in scoring systems for CHD.<SEP>Blood Pressure is a measurable vital sign, the control of which is central to the report's recommendations.

10-year Risk for CHD
The 10-year risk for CHD is divided into three categories: >20%, 10-20%, and <10%, assessed by Framingham scoring.

Core Risk Status
The core risk status is the initial risk assessment determined by major risk factors in ATP III.

Life-Habit Risk Factors
These are direct targets for clinical intervention and include obesity, physical inactivity, and atherogenic diet.

Emerging Risk Factors
These risk factors include lipoprotein (a), homocysteine, and proinflammatory factors, and they contribute to CHD risk.

Primary Prevention
Primary prevention is a public health approach aimed at reducing the overall burden of CHD through lifestyle changes.

Obesity
Obesity is a life-habit risk factor and a characteristic component of the Metabolic Syndrome.

Physical Inactivity
Physical inactivity is a life-habit risk factor that contributes to CHD risk.

Atherogenic Diet
Atherogenic diet is a life-habit risk factor influencing CHD risk.

Homocysteine
Homocysteine is an emerging risk factor that contributes to the risk of CHD.

Impaired Fasting Glucose
Impaired fasting glucose is an emerging risk factor contributing to CHD risk.

Abdominal Obesity
Abdominal obesity is a factor characteristic of the Metabolic Syndrome.

Atherogenic Dyslipidemia
Atherogenic dyslipidemia is characterized by elevated triglyceride, small LDL particles, and low HDL cholesterol, defining the Metabolic Syndrome.

Insulin Resistance
Insulin resistance, with or without glucose intolerance, is a factor characteristic of the Metabolic Syndrome.

Weight Control
Weight control is an essential lifestyle change recommended for primary prevention to reduce CHD risk.

Drug Therapy
TLC and drug therapy are presented as two major modalities for LDL-lowering therapy.

Major Risk Factors
Multiple major risk factors, excluding LDL cholesterol, are used to modify the goals for LDL-lowering treatment.

Risk Category
The ATP III guidelines use three defined risk categories to determine the goals and modalities for LDL-lowering therapy.

United States
Congress enacted public laws mandating that no person in the United States shall be subjected to discrimination.<SEP>The United States is the geographical area where various health statistics, including mortality and prevalence rates, are reported.

Multiple Risk Factors
The presence of multiple risk factors (2+) modifies the LDL cholesterol goal, setting the target at <130 mg/dL.

CHD risk equivalents
The category of highest risk is defined by CHD and other CHD risk equivalents, which carry a major risk for coronary events.

NCEP Expert Panel
The NCEP Expert Panel conducted studies on the detection, evaluation, and treatment of high blood cholesterol.

100 mg/dL
If the baseline LDL cholesterol is less than 100 mg/dL, further LDL-lowering therapy is not required.

Older Persons
Clinical judgment is needed for older persons (65 years) to determine the appropriate intensity of treatment based on overall health status.

Drug Use
The use of drugs is linked to the possibility of developing Peptic Ulcer Disease.

World Athletics Championship
The World Athletics Championship is a global sports competition featuring top athletes in track and field.

Tokyo
Tokyo is the host city of the World Athletics Championship.

Noah Carter
Noah Carter is a sprinter who set a new record in the 100m sprint at the World Athletics Championship.

100m Sprint Record
The 100m sprint record is a benchmark in athletics, recently broken by Noah Carter.

Carbon-Fiber Spikes
Carbon-fiber spikes are advanced sprinting shoes that provide enhanced speed and traction.

World Athletics Federation
The World Athletics Federation is the governing body overseeing the World Athletics Championship and record validations.

Global Tech Index
The Global Tech Index tracks the performance of major technology stocks and experienced a 3.4% decline today.

Nexon Technologies
Nexon Technologies is a tech company that saw its stock decline by 7.8% after disappointing earnings.

Omega Energy
Omega Energy is an energy company that gained 2.1% in stock value due to rising oil prices.

Gold Futures
Gold futures rose by 1.5%, indicating increased investor interest in safe-haven assets.

Crude Oil
Crude oil prices rose to $87.60 per barrel due to supply constraints and strong demand.

Market Selloff
Market selloff refers to the significant decline in stock values due to investor concerns over interest rates and regulations.

Federal Reserve Policy Announcement
The Federal Reserve's upcoming policy announcement is expected to impact investor confidence and market stability.

oldpeak
A measured variable representing ST depression induced by exercise relative to rest.

slope
The slope of the peak exercise ST segment, which can be upsloping, flat, or downsloping.

rldv5
A measurement representing the height of a vessel at rest.

rldv5e
A measurement representing the height of a vessel at peak exercise.

ca
A variable indicating the number of major vessels (0-3) colored by fluoroscopy.

restef
A measurement representing rest radio nuclid ejection fraction.

restwm
A measurement representing rest wall motion abnormality, categorized from none to akinesis or dyskmem.

exeref
A measurement representing exercise radio nuclid ejection fraction.

exerwm
A measurement representing exercise wall motion.

thal
A variable classifying defects, noting normal, fixed defect, or reversible defect.

cmo
The month of cardiac catheterization.

cday
The day of cardiac catheterization.

cyr
The year of cardiac catheterization.

num
The diagnosis status of heart disease based on diameter

ladprox
A measurement variable representing the proximal diameter of the Left Anterior Descending artery.

laddist
A measurement variable representing the distance related to the Left Anterior Descending artery.

diag
A measurement variable representing the diagnosis status of a major vessel.

cxmain
A measurement variable representing the main Coronary artery.

ramus
A measurement variable representing the Ramus coronary vessel.

om1
A measurement variable representing the first observed major coronary vessel.

om2
A measurement variable representing the second observed major coronary vessel.

rcaprox
A measurement variable representing the proximal diameter of the Right Coronary artery.

rcadist
A measurement variable representing the distance related to the Right Coronary artery.

Class Distribution Data
Structured data showing the distribution of patient classifications (Cleveland, Hungarian, Switzerland, Long Beach VA) across various class categories (0 through 4).

Cleveland Clinic Foundation
The Cleveland Clinic Foundation is one of the institutions contributing data for heart disease diagnosis.

Hungarian Institute Of Cardiology
The Hungarian Institute of Cardiology is a contributor of data located in Budapest.

V.A. Medical Center, Long Beach
The V.A. Medical Center is an institution located in Long Beach, CA, contributing data for heart disease diagnosis.

University Hospital, Zurich
The University Hospital is an institution located in Zurich, Switzerland, contributing data.

Heart Disease Diagnosis
This is the medical condition for which the collected databases contain attributes and are used for diagnosis.

Data Set
The data sets consist of 76 raw attributes, often reduced to 14 attributes used for experimental diagnosis.

David W. Aha
David W. Aha is identified as the donor of the data collection.

Andras Janosi
Andras Janosi is the principal investigator responsible for data collection at the Hungarian Institute of Cardiology.

William Steinbrunn
William Steinbrunn is the principal investigator responsible for data collection at the University Hospital in Zurich.

Matthias Pfisterer
Matthias Pfisterer is the principal investigator responsible for data collection at the University Hospital in Basel.

Robert Detrano
Robert Detrano is the principal investigator responsible for data collection at the V.A. Medical Center and Cleveland Clinic Foundation.

University Hospital, Basel
The University Hospital is an institution in Basel, Switzerland, associated with Matthias Pfisterer.

76 Raw Attributes
The total raw attributes available in the databases, from which only 14 were utilized for experiments.

14 Used Attributes
A subset of attributes, specifically 14 of them, that were actually utilized in past experimental diagnoses.

Sex
The biological sex of the patient, categorized as 1 for male and 0 for female.

Chest Pain Type (Cp)
Describes the type of chest pain, with possible values including typical angina, atypical angina, non-anginal pain, or asymptomatic.

Resting Blood Pressure (Trestbps)
The patient's resting blood pressure measured in mm Hg upon hospital admission.

Serum Cholesterol (Chol)
The level of serum cholesterol measured in mg/dl.

Fasting Blood Sugar (Fbs)
Indicates whether the patient's fasting blood sugar is greater than 120 mg/dl.

Resting Electrocardiographic Results (Restecg)
The results observed from a resting electrocardiogram, including normal, ST-T wave abnormalities, or evidence of left ventricular hypertrophy.

Maximum Heart Rate Achieved (Thalach)
The maximum heart rate the patient achieved during exercise testing.

Exercise Induced Angina (Exang)
Indicates whether the patient experienced angina triggered by exercise.

Oldpeak
Represents the amount of ST depression induced by exercise relative to resting values.

Slope
Describes the slope of the peak exercise ST segment, which can be upsloping, flat, or downsloping.

Major Vessels (Ca)
The count, ranging from 0 to 3, of major vessels colored by fluoroscopy.

Pain Location
Defines the location of chest pain, where values include substernal and other locations.

Chest Pain Exertion
Indicates whether chest pain is provoked by physical exertion.

Relief After Rest
Records whether chest pain is relieved after resting.

History Of Diabetes
Indicates if the patient has a history of diabetes (1 = yes, 0 = no).

Smoking History
Documents smoking status, including smoke status (yes/no), cigarettes smoked per day, and years as a smoker.

Family History
Records the family history of coronary artery disease.

Digitalis Use
Indicates if digitalis was used during an exercise ECG.

Exercise Protocol
Defines the type of exercise protocol used during a cardiac test, with specific options like Bruce or Balke.

Beta Blocker Use
Indicates if a beta blocker was used during an exercise ECG.

Nitrate Use
Indicates if nitrates were used during an exercise ECG.

Calcium Channel Blocker Use
Indicates if a calcium channel blocker was used during an exercise ECG.

Diuretic Use
Indicates if a diuretic was used during an exercise ECG.

Exercise Blood Pressure
Represents peak exercise blood pressure measured in two parts (tpeakbps and tpeakbpd).

Concept
The value of fasting blood sugar is used to determine the patient's history of elevated blood sugar.

Exercise Induced Angina
Exercise induced angina is a key indicator used in diagnosing chest pain issues.

Philip B. Gorelik

data.entity_name: Joint National Committee On Prevention, Detection, Evaluation, And Treatment Of High Blood Pressure
data.content:
Joint National Committee On Prevention, Detection, Evaluation, And Treatment Of High Blood Pressure
The committee is responsible for generating reports and guidelines regarding the prevention, detection, evaluation, and treatment of high blood pressure.<SEP>The official body responsible for compiling the landmark report on high blood pressure.

High Blood Pressure
High Blood Pressure is a primary condition monitored by the committee, with trends in its awareness, treatment, and control tracked across decades.<SEP>High Blood Pressure, also referred to as hypertension, is the public health issue addressed by the report.

Ambulatory Blood Pressure Monitoring
This method is a clinical situation suggested for helping in the assessment of blood pressure.

Cardiovascular Risk Factors
Cardiovascular Risk Factors are a set of elements that contribute to the risk of cardiovascular diseases and are analyzed by the committee.

These tests are recommended for identifying causes of hypertension.

Oral Antihypertensive Drugs
This content details various medications that can be administered orally for the treatment of hypertension.

Combination Drugs For Hypertension
This content details drug classes that can be used in combination for treating hypertension.

This data point provides benchmarks for blood pressure levels across selected ages, heights, and genders in children and adolescents.

data.entity_name: Laboratory Tests And Other Diagnostic Procedures
data.content:
Laboratory Tests And Other Diagnostic Procedures
These procedures are necessary for diagnosing and monitoring the progression of hypertension.

Genetics Of Hypertension
Genetics of Hypertension addresses the inherited or hereditary factors contributing to the condition.

Treatment
Treatment describes the overall strategy implemented to manage hypertension.

Blood Pressure Control Rates
Blood Pressure Control Rates are key metrics used to assess the effectiveness of hypertension management.

Goals Of Therapy
Goals of Therapy outline the specific objectives that must be achieved during the management of hypertension.

Lifestyle Modifications
Lifestyle Modifications are non-pharmacological interventions recommended for treating hypertension.<SEP>Lifestyle modifications are recognized as a key factor in promoting better patient outcomes and improving blood pressure.

Pharmacologic Treatment
Pharmacologic Treatment involves the use of medication to control hypertension.

Thiazide-Type Diuretics
Thiazide-Type Diuretics are recommended as the preferred initial drug agent for treating hypertension.

Special Situations In Hypertension Management
This section covers specific management considerations required when hypertension coexists with other diseases.

Ischemic Heart Disease
Ischemic Heart Disease is a specific condition that must be considered in the management of hypertension.

Heart Failure
Heart Failure is a specific comorbidity that requires careful consideration during hypertension management.

Diabetes And Hypertension
This concept addresses the dual management requirements when a patient suffers from both diabetes and hypertension.

Chronic Kidney Disease
Chronic Kidney Disease is a specific comorbidity that influences the treatment strategy for hypertension.

Patients With Cerebrovascular Disease
This concept covers management protocols for hypertension in individuals who have cerebrovascular disease.

Age Trends
Age Trends track the prevalence and risk factors of hypertension across different age groups.

Clinical Impact
Clinical Impact measures the severity and real-world consequences of uncontrolled hypertension.

Lipids
This sub-topic focuses specifically on the management and implications of lipids in the context of metabolic syndrome.<SEP>Lipids are a subject area within cardiovascular risk assessment, related to hypertension management.

Blood Pressure (BP)
Blood Pressure is a physiological measurement, with metrics including Median SBP (Systolic Blood Pressure) and DBP (Diastolic Blood Pressure), used to assess cardiovascular health.

Stroke is a disease whose age-adjusted death rates declined in the United States between 1970 and 2000.

IHD is a risk factor for death throughout the world, associated with suboptimal blood pressure levels.

data.entity_name: World Morbidity and Mortality attributed to hypertension
data.content:
World Morbidity and Mortality attributed to hypertension
This data shows that hypertension is responsible for significant percentages of cerebrovascular and ischemic heart disease deaths globally.

Table 1
Table 1 presents detailed trends regarding awareness, treatment, and control of high blood pressure in the United States between 1976 and 2000.

140/90 mmHg
The threshold defining high blood pressure, measured as a systolic blood pressure (SBP) of 140 mmHg or greater and a diastolic blood pressure (DBP) of 90 mmHg or greater.<SEP>140/90 mmHg is a specific blood pressure reading that the Joint National Committee on Prevention, Detection, Evaluation, and Treatment of High Blood Pressure referenced for undiagnosed hypertension.

115 mmHg SBP
The systolic blood pressure (SBP) level below which a condition is classified as suboptimal, specifically noted when above 115 mmHg.

data.entity_name: Joint National Committee On Prevention, Detection, Evaluation, And Treatment Of High Blood Pressure (JNC 7)
data.content:
Joint National Committee On Prevention, Detection, Evaluation, And Treatment Of High Blood Pressure (JNC 7)

data.entity_name: National High Blood Pressure Education Program (NHBPEP) Coordinating Committee
data.content:
National High Blood Pressure Education Program (NHBPEP) Coordinating Committee
The committee was involved in the production of the JNC 7 report and coordinating related efforts.

Goal Blood Pressure
The optimal blood pressure goals are defined as <140/90 mmHg or <130/80 mmHg for diabetics.

JNC 7 Guidelines
The JNC 7 guidelines offer evidence-based recommendations to clinicians and the public regarding blood pressure management.

Primary Care Clinicians
Primary Care Clinicians are professionals who are the intended audience for the JNC 7 report and guidelines.

Public Health Workers
Public Health Workers are another intended audience for the JNC 7 report, helping with the prevention of hypertension.

Anti-Hypertensive Drug Classes
A collection of drug types recommended for managing hypertension, including ACE inhibitors, ARBs, and CCBs.

Barbara M. Alving, M.D.
Barbara M. Alving, M.D. is a professional whose name is mentioned in the context of the JNC 7 committee discussions.

Coronary Heart Disease
Coronary Heart Disease is a health condition whose age-adjusted mortality rates were tracked in the United States between 1970 and 2000.

Systolic Blood Pressure (SBP)
SBP measures the pressure in the arteries when the heart beats, and the guidelines set goals for maintaining this measure.

Diastolic Blood Pressure (DBP)
DBP measures the pressure in the arteries when the heart is at rest, and the guidelines set goals for maintaining this measure.

Congestive Heart Failure
Congestive heart failure is a health condition whose case-fatality rates and prevalence were studied in the United States.

End-Stage Renal Disease (ESRD)
ESRD is a health condition exhibiting an increasing trend, linked to uncontrolled hypertension.

Age-Adjusted Mortality Rates
Age-adjusted mortality rates are the metrics used to measure the decline of coronary heart disease in the United States.

Prevalence
Prevalence is a metric tracking the occurrence of congestive heart failure and is reported by race and gender.

Figure 3
Figure 3 is a chart displaying the percent decline in age-adjusted mortality rates for coronary heart disease by gender and race in the United States from 1970 to 2000.

Figure 4
Figure 4 is a chart displaying hospital case-fatality rates for congestive heart failure for ages younger than 65 years and 65 years and older in the United States from 1981 to 2000.

data.content:
Clinical Management Of The Metabolic Syndrome

This topic discusses the condition of hypertrophy in the left ventricle.

This topic addresses the specific medical condition characterized by resistant hypertension.

Hypertensive Crises: Emergencies And Urgencies

Erectile Dysfunction And Hypertension
This topic details the relationship between hypertension and the occurrence of erectile dysfunction.

Urinary Outflow Obstruction
This topic addresses the management of urinary outflow obstruction.

Patients Undergoing Surgery

Dental Issues In Hypertensive Individuals
This topic discusses the oral health challenges and implications for individuals with hypertension.

Obstructive Sleep Apnea

Hypertension And The Eye

Renal Transplantation

Patients With Renovascular Disease

This topic provides general strategies and methods for achieving optimal control of hypertension.

This topic highlights the necessary involvement and contributions of various health care professionals in patient management.

Economic Barriers
This topic discusses financial constraints and economic hurdles in accessing hypertension care.

natural history
The age-adjusted death rate for stroke showed a decline in the United States between 1970 and 2000.

NHBPEP Coordinating Committee

Health Care
The untreated and uncontrolled hypertension places a substantial strain on the health care delivery system.
